old_claim.note: Anatomy (Early Studies) is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency is retained because Classical sculpture used artistic observation and workshop practice rather than formal Alexandrian anatomy as a prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: High Classical sculpture section discusses 5th-century BCE sculptural style, anatomical competence, Phidias, Polyclitus, and Parthenon sculpture.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Britannica anchors classical sculpture in 5th-century BCE Greek artistic practice, while anatomical science as patched here is anchored to later Alexandrian medical dissection.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Artistic anatomical competence is not the same as formal anatomical studies by Herophilus; treating the latter as a predecessor forces later medical chronology onto earlier Greek sculpture.

ontology_before: The sculpture node treated early anatomy studies as a historical predecessor, creating a false dependence on later Hellenistic medical anatomy.
ontology_after: Classical sculpture remains connected to masonry, bronze working, philosophy, geometry, and pigment creation, which are sufficient for its material and aesthetic foundations.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant changed: Classical sculpture must not directly depend on Alexandrian anatomy unless the node is narrowed to later anatomy-informed art theory.
would_reject_if: A source shows that the scoped Classical Sculpture node intentionally means post-Herophilus anatomy-informed sculpture.
The anatomy node is rescoped to broad bodily observation before the High Classical period with source-backed chronology.
A source demonstrates direct institutional transmission from anatomical dissection to Classical sculptural practice.
short_reason: Formal Alexandrian anatomy is later than Classical sculpture and should not be modeled as its prerequisite.
